message.content:
Heydon, all three answers are clear and helpful. Let me confirm my updated design direction for each. ## 1. Inline Count Badges -- Plain Text, Agreed Simple version adopted: ```html Inbox (3) ``` The `` gives me the CSS hook I need (smaller font, muted color) without any ARIA complexity. "Inbox (3), link" is exactly what sighted users see and what screen readers announce. No divergence risk, no maintenance trap. Done. ## 2. Two-Tier Hierarchy -- Weight Over Color Your caveat is well-taken. I'll use **font-weight** as the primary de-emphasis tool instead of color: - Tier 1 (Inbox, Next Actions, Projects): `--font-semibold` (600) - Tier 2 (Waiting For, Someday/Maybe, Review): `--font-normal` (400) - All links use the same color (`--color-fg-muted` at rest, `--color-fg` when active) This gives clear visual hierarchy with zero contrast risk. Weight differences are immediately perceptible to sighted users but don't change the contrast ratio at all. Better solution than what I originally proposed. ## 3. Clarify Flow -- Major Design Correction Accepted Thank you for catching this. My card-based one-question-at-a-time design was wrong -- it assumed HTMX swaps between decision steps, which is the wizard pattern you and Steve Krug rejected. **Updated design understanding:** The clarify flow is a **single form with progressive disclosure via CSS `:has()`**. Here's how I'm now thinking about the visual design: **Page structure:** ``` Process inbox item 4 of 12 remaining Back to inbox +------------------------------------------+ | "Call dentist about appointment" | | Captured 2 hours ago | +------------------------------------------+ Is this actionable? ( ) Yes ( ) No [If "Yes" selected -- revealed via :has()] What type? ( ) Next action ( ) Project ( ) Delegate [If "Next action" -- revealed via nested :has()] Title: [___________________________] Context: @computer @home @errands @phone @anywhere [If "No" selected -- revealed via :has()] ( ) Trash ( ) Someday/Maybe [ Clarify ] Skip ``` **Key design differences from my earlier proposal:** - The decision buttons become **radio buttons**, not large action buttons. Radio buttons are the right semantic element for "pick one option." - Conditional fields **appear instantly** below the selected radio, no network round-trip, no loading state. - One **"Clarify" submit button** at the bottom of the form (not per-step action buttons). - "Skip" as a secondary text link, not a button. - The captured item stays visible at the top throughout -- it's the context for all decisions below. **Visual treatment of radio groups:** - Radio buttons with clear, visible labels. No custom styled radio buttons that break a11y -- use native radios with CSS that enhances their appearance. - Each radio group has a visible fieldset legend ("Is this actionable?", "What type?"). - Conditionally revealed sections have a slight left indent or subtle left border to show they're dependent on the parent choice. **The only HTMX interaction:** Form submit -> server processes -> returns the NEXT inbox item's clarify page. Focus moves to `

`, aria-live announces "Moved to Next Actions. Processing item 5 of 12." This is simpler, more accessible, and works without JS out of the box. The CSS `:has()` is progressive enhancement -- without it, all fields are visible (which still works, just noisier). One question: for the `:has()` progressive enhancement, should the no-JS fallback show all fields visible with a note like "Select an option above to see relevant fields"? Or should we just show everything and let users fill in what's relevant?

content.text: Steve Schoger's updated design is excellent. He's fully corrected course on the clarify flow and his visual treatment is thoughtful. His question about the no-JS fallback is a good one.

input.content:
Steve, that updated clarify design is spot-on. The visual structure is clear, the radio groups with fieldset legends are correct, and the left indent for conditional sections is a nice visual affordance that communicates dependency without relying on ARIA.

A few notes on your design, then the answer to your fallback question:

## Native Radio Buttons -- Exactly Right

Your instinct to use native radios with CSS enhancement (not custom-styled replacements) is correct. Custom radio buttons are one of the most common sources of a11y bugs -- developers replace the native element with a styled `<div>` and forget to wire up keyboard behavior, focus indicators, and ARIA states. Native `<input type="radio">` gives you all of that for free. You can style the label and the surrounding area as much as you want, and use CSS `accent-color` to tint the native radio to match your design tokens.

## Fieldset/Legend -- One Nuance

Using `<fieldset>` with `<legend>` for each radio group is the correct semantic pattern. One nuance: screen readers announce the legend text before every radio button in the group. So if the legend is "Is this actionable?" and the options are "Yes" and "No," a screen reader user hears:

- "Is this actionable? Yes, radio button, 1 of 2"
- "Is this actionable? No, radio button, 2 of 2"

This repetition is expected and helpful -- it provides context. But keep legend text short. "Is this actionable?" is good. "Please select whether this inbox item represents something you need to take action on" is too long and will be announced before every option.

## "Skip" as a Link -- Correct

"Skip" as a secondary text link (not a button) is right. Skipping doesn't modify data -- it navigates to the next item. Links are for navigation, buttons are for actions. The link should go to `/inbox/{next-id}/clarify` or back to `/inbox` depending on the product decision. I'd suggest it goes to the next item (consistent with the flow-through pattern Carson designed).

## Your Fallback Question: Show Everything, No Note

For the no-JS / no-`:has()` fallback: **show all fields visible, no explanatory note.**

Here's why:
- The form still works. The user sees all the fields, selects the relevant radio buttons, fills in the relevant text inputs, and submits. The server ignores fields that don't apply to the selected option (server-side validation handles this).
- An explanatory note like "Select an option above to see relevant fields" is confusing when all fields ARE visible. It describes behavior that isn't happening.
- Users who don't have `:has()` support are on older browsers. They're accustomed to seeing longer forms. It's "noisier" as you said, but it's functional. That's what progressive enhancement means -- the baseline works, the enhancement makes it nicer.
- The fieldset legends and radio group labels already communicate which fields go with which choice. The form structure itself is the instruction.

The one thing the server MUST handle: if the user selects "No" (not actionable) but also fills in the "Next action title" field (because it was visible in the fallback), the server ignores the next action fields entirely. The radio button selection is the authoritative signal. This is standard form handling -- the server already needs to parse based on the selected option, not blindly accept all fields.

## Overall

Your updated design is clean, accessible, and aligned with the team's consensus. The single-form structure with radio groups, fieldset/legend, and CSS `:has()` progressive disclosure is going to be one of the best-designed clarify flows in any GTD app. And it'll work without JavaScript. That's not something most commercial GTD apps can say.
input.summary: Design confirmed, native radios, short legends, show-all fallback, no explanatory note

  }

content.text:
I've responded to Steve Schoger's updated clarify flow design with four specific points:

1. **Native radio buttons**: Confirmed his instinct. Use native `<input type="radio">` with CSS `accent-color`, not custom-styled replacements.

2. **Fieldset/legend nuance**: Keep legend text short because screen readers announce it before every radio option in the group. "Is this actionable?" is good; verbose descriptions are not.

3. **"Skip" as a link**: Confirmed correct -- links for navigation, buttons for actions. Skip should go to the next inbox item's clarify page.

4. **No-JS fallback**: Show all fields visible, no explanatory note. The form structure is self-documenting via fieldset/legend. The server ignores irrelevant fields based on the radio button selection (the authoritative signal).

All my review work for the product discovery task is complete. I'm standing by for the consensus finalization and the transition to implementation tasks.
